To to the marrow and I would be there helping him. And yeah, it was it was. I saw it when it was nothing. And I saw it become something. And he was. Well, his whole, his whole expression about the West Indian Club was pure pride. You know, it was it was really a sense of his heritage and a place that he had a lot of pride for a lot. Um, he just it was just something that he was very proud of. That's not the West Indian club I grew up with, you know, the West Indian club I grew up in, they had talent shows. And, um, there was a woman, Mrs. Alexander, and she and I don't remember who else it was, but they used to, uh, charter a bus and take us kids to New York to plays and museums and, um, all these things like every year, you know, I, I remember being from the time I was like maybe 10 or 11 to probably 12 or 13, we went on these bus rides and it would take us to a different museum. They would take us. I remember going to see me main mean main, main, the show with Lucille Ball, her Broadway, the show that was on Broadway with her. Um, they really tried to give us a sense of not just our culture, but the culture of the country that we lived in. Mhm. And, and, and took us to places outside of what we, we would normally experience in probably every family couldn't do all of these things. But because they took the initiative, we would have a bus full of kids, you know? Yeah. With chaperones in New York. Learning new things. So the West Indian club that I grew up with was a phenomenal place. It really, I, I'm like, I, I wish my nieces and nephews knew the club that I know. Yeah. So when I think when the West Indian club was not feeling like the West Indian club that he was a part of, they were still a part of the West Indian club that was doing the work that he was proud of. It. He was proud of. And then. So you have your father's generation. You have your generation. And from what I can, from what I can tell from from talking to the men in your father's generation, they truly believe that if they could earn what they earned and have the restrictions they had, meaning, you know, they have two jobs. That it would only get better, right? This is not even my West Indian club is what people are saying. Right? It really isn't. And in a lot of ways, it it's kind of hurtful because when you do think of what they accomplished with what they, the meager things that they had, it's like, how do we let this fall? You know, how do we, how do we not step up to the plate and make this work? But I think there came a time with all the professionals. It became more about money than about the spirit of what the West Indian Club was created for. And everybody. At one point in time, I think the West Indian club was, had become more like a cash cow because anybody from the West Indies that was anybody would want to perform at the West Indian Club, you know. You know, they could have they could get any, almost any celebrity that they want. And so it became a way for people to make money. And with the money and with it, the it kind of they, they didn't really have um. The same principles, you know. So when it became about money and then it became a place that became violent because you got all different type of elements that came up in there. And then whenever you were going to West Indian Club, when you started having tight, tight security, you know, when that's the catch phrase for coming to a club. Then you know, the club is heading downhill because. Mhm. You shouldn't pay your money to be in danger. I'm sorry. You know. Um, and that that kind of started as I was, I don't know, I don't I'm not sure that it started while I was in high school. I think that was more like when I was in college and I would come back home. That's when it was really became all about. And the people that had a good heart or good intentions for the club, they were overshadowed by the people that felt like, oh, the club can make, you know, if they made this much money, they would be set. But then the money wasn't going in the right places to make the club set. You know it. Um, and then the people that had the best intentions for the church. The, the, the club, they got disenchanted and the people that really wanted to see it flourish and keep the legacy going. Those are the people that walked away. And then a lot of the people that stayed were the people that were, you know, leeching off of it. And then when there was nothing left to Leach, they were gone. And the few people that were remained just felt like they couldn't get the good people back anymore. Mhm. So it was like, it was really a sad thing to see. Um, and my, my dad was never the thing about my dad. He was never, he never was a president of anything, you know? Right. Wasn't wrong. He never. That's not how he saw him. So he. He would tell you, I'm not a business person. That's not who he is, but he's a person that can get something done and. Right. You don't need a title to get things done. And so the business people that we're supposed to be handling the business, they didn't have, they no longer had the same, same values. Right? Yeah. I mean, and not and, and your father, you know, the things that you're remembering your father for, he knows what he's, he knows what he's good at. And he put his effort and energy into the things that he's good at. And there's some people who are good at being project managers and really good at finance and really good at, you know, compliance and making sure everything is, um, legal and up to date and, um, and that the club no longer has such a huge membership that it can call on all of those types of personalities, um, to do the business of the club. But, um, since you've been gone, you know, there's been a lot of soul searching and, um, and the pandemic has also, um, made everybody do a lot of soul searching about, okay. If, if we actually shut down all of our activities is that does the club actually have a purpose? And I think some of the things that you're talking about, um, are back on the agenda where people are realizing that, um, it's a different time and the club needs to reconsider, um, its founding fathers, their mission, their vision and figure out what, what, what the vision is, you know, for 2022. Um, and, and beyond. And I'm optimistic. I love, I would love to be a part of that new club atmosphere. My brother, we haven't really talked very much about my mother, my birth mom's side of the family, but my seven brothers and all seven of us are here in, in, in Connecticut. Oh, wow. Okay. We all live in Connecticut. And my brother Tony, he he was a council councilman when he was in his early 30s. And he was the registrar of voters since I think 95 and until he retired. Mean, but it's usually, you know, most people know him as Tony. And he was the spokesperson for the Archdiocese of ISS Asylum Hill for several years too. So he has been active in, you know, um, he's been active in Hartford politics since I was 15, 16 because I used to go to functions with him and he had, um, he was a part of that next generation that tried to jump in and be a part of the West Indian club. But then he recognized it wasn't doing what he thought it should do. And he kind of like went, you know, separated himself from it. But I, I, I've known people that have wanted to see it, you know. Prosper, prosper. You know, I want to see it prosper. I, I would gladly become a member, even though I don't still live in Connecticut, I would still do whatever I could to support it because It's. It's the blood that's in my veins. I, I was at the West Indian Club as much as my dad. Yeah. Um, it was like, it's the blood that's in my veins. I know what that meant to him. I'm so grateful for Miss Johnson for doing the interviews that she did. Right. But that was just a handful. That was just a handful of, um, of people. And I think, I think somewhere in the back of your mind, you know, you think as the, as the community gets bigger, you think that these projects will happen and then everybody is waiting for somebody else to do it or think somebody else is going to do it. And then, you know, you blink and those stories are gone and the generation is gone. So you're already, you're already helping, um, by doing that. So don't, don't be shocked if you end up on a podcast or if you, um, or if you end up, you know, I'm going to see what we can do about that documentary. Um, of course, and also just to create the archive, right? People like your father, they saved everything. They saved everything that they thought was important. And I think one of the things that we can do now is that use all of the technology that's available to us to try to at least preserve these. Um, try to preserve these stories, right. So that your, your, your nieces and your cousins can like, read, read about your father, right? And, and about you and about you as well. He was the first black person to hold the position of, um, the section supervisor for the city of Hartford, um, parks department. And that was a two year long campaign that Mr. Bennett had to help us get because they said they hired him as an unskilled laborer. And although he had been doing the job for ten years, they didn't want to give him the title. So, you know, there are. Oh, God, there are stories. You know, there's perseverance, there's endurance, there's is resilience in these stories. And if you come after and you you're benefiting from us, you're benefiting now from me not being liked as a Jamaican kids, when I grew up, kids would go, oh, you're not black, you're Jamaican. I was like, okay, so if the Klu Klux Klan comes to kill all the black people, make sure you tell because I always have, you know? And I actually saw a Klu Klux Klan rally in Hartford, if you can believe it or not. I saw them marching through Main Street. I tell them, like, if the Ku Klux Klan come to kill the black people, make sure you tell them she's not black, she's Jamaican, you know? So it's those kind of things that when you come now and we're revered and we're loved and we've done this, that and the other. It wasn't always like that, you know. Mhm. It wasn't always like that. And I and even from the the West Indian side, I was too American to be Jamaican and to Jamaican to be American. And I to this day, I still have people tell me, well, you're not really Jamaican. I'm like, okay, if you're born in Jamaica and both of your parents are Jamaican and you're not Jamaican, what makes you Jamaican? What's the criteria? Right? MM. You know, like, what's the criteria? Please explain it to me. Um, so there are things that I just feel that we can't allow to be lost for sure. I think, um, I keep thinking which part of your interview am I going to clip for the podcast? And I feel like the podcast is going to be like two hours because I want to clip all of it. I think. Uh. No. Thank you. No. Thank you. No, I really, I really, I really appreciate it. And you're totally, like, writing my script for me. All I'm going to do is just put quotes around it around, you know, put quotes around it. Can you say a little bit about, about your, your, your dad's, um, role role and what that two year battle was, was like to become the supervisor? Oh, sure. Yes, yes, yes, yes. And I'm, I'm, I'm currently going, trying to go through the archives of the Hartford Courant because I knew that I had a hard copy of like two different, um, uh, newspaper articles that was out, but I'm having difficulty finding it. But. Well, he had been, he had worked for the Park Department, the parks department, um, managing um, Northwood Cemetery and the other cemeteries throughout Hartford. And his, uh, his boss had, uh, retired maybe about seven years earlier. And then the assistant boss, the boss's assistant, he had retired maybe about three years after that. So my father had been doing this job. Even when he had those bosses, he was doing the job of organizing and managing these cemeteries. But when the all of the management left, it just left him there as what they considered unskilled labor to manage and run this these cemeteries. And then what happened is he was now about ready to retire too. And so he well, first of all, he asked for the promotion and they told them they could not give him the promotion because he was hired as unskilled labor. Mhm. And so since he was about to retire, he was like, okay, no problem. He'll just retire. But then they came back to him and said, oh, we're going to send Joe Schmo over for you to teach them what you've done. Mhm. Um, and how you manage things. And by this time, I'm grown like I'm in college and I'm like, no, no. If you're too dumb to hold the position, then you're too dumb to to teach somebody how to do it. And because. Um, that's what it boils down to, you know? So, um, and my dad and I had a really good relationship. Like, I listened to his advice and he listens, listened to my advice. Mhm. So he, um, so I told him if they ever came Warning. They said the so and so's coming. You had tons of vacation days, sick days. He had like three years of paid leave. I'm like, take the day off, go to the doctor, whatever, but don't be there. And so we played this cat and mouse game for a while. And then, um, he said, well, he wanted the position if he was going to teach somebody what he wanted the position. And they said, well, you have to take a test. So he took the test. He passed the test, but then they lost the test and they kept while they lost the test, they were still trying to send somebody in for him to train. So they were having these stall tactics to try to get him to train someone. So then, um, they decided that, okay, well, you have to take another test. And then by that time we had taken it to Mr. Bennett. Mhm. Um. and through his contacts, he had, uh, he had taken it to the paper, you know, and then the next test. Hallelujah. He's a brilliant man. And we're promoting him. And he became the first section supervisor for the Parks Department. And but it was like 2 to 3 years of going backwards and forwards and them trying to slip somebody in for him to train. But because he held his guns and didn't train anyone, they had no choice but to promote him into the position because nobody else knew what he did. Right. There was nobody else that had been doing this to, to, to hand it off to. And he was retired for 30 years, if you can imagine. He he lived to be 95. He was retired for 30 years. And he told me He said, I have never regretted listening to you because I because I told him, he said, oh, it's not worth the fight. I said, daddy, if it wasn't something special about this, they would have given it to you. The fact that they want to fight you and not give it to you, there's something in here, there that they don't want you to have. And lo and behold, it was because every year when the city of Hartford got a pay increase, he got the pay increase. He had much better health and benefits because he got this promotion than he would have had he retired as an unskilled laborer. So there was so much benefit for him taking the fight and standing up for what was right. And he he lived to thank me. Yeah. That's fantastic. Let's pause for one second.

lines.line: # Early Childhood on Garden Street and Schooling

> The speaker recalls her early years living on Garden Street, attending Vine Street School, and forming lasting friendships, such as with Yvonne Parker. She describes the context of desegregation and the experience of being bussed to another school, which was both frightening and formative. The narrative sets the stage for her family's life and the broader social changes occurring in Hartford during her childhood.

metadata.title: Early Childhood on Garden Street and Schooling
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls her early years living on Garden Street, attending Vine Street School, and forming lasting friendships, such as with Yvonne Parker. She describes the context of desegregation and the experience of being bussed to another school, which was both frightening and formative. The narrative sets the stage for her family's life and the broader social changes occurring in Hartford during her childhood.

lines.line: # Father’s Involvement with the West Indian Social Club

> The speaker describes her father's deep commitment to the West Indian Social Club, where he spent much of his free time building, fixing, and organizing events. The club was central to his social life, and the family often hosted visiting bands and participated in club activities. The speaker also touches on her stepmother's social network, which revolved around card games and bingo, and her supportive but less public role in club affairs.

metadata.title: Father’s Involvement with the West Indian Social Club
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes her father's deep commitment to the West Indian Social Club, where he spent much of his free time building, fixing, and organizing events. The club was central to his social life, and the family often hosted visiting bands and participated in club activities. The speaker also touches on her stepmother's social network, which revolved around card games and bingo, and her supportive but less public role in club affairs.

lines.line: # The Bussing Incident and Move to Holcomb Street

> A traumatic incident occurred when the speaker, only five years old, was dropped off at the wrong location by a bus driver, leaving her stranded and frightened. An elderly woman helped her return home, and this event led her parents to withdraw her from the bussed school. The family was in the process of moving to Holcomb Street, which allowed her to attend a new, predominantly white school without the need for bussing.

metadata.title: The Bussing Incident and Move to Holcomb Street

An elderly woman helped her return home, and this event led her parents to withdraw her from the bussed school. The family was in the process of moving to Holcomb Street, which allowed her to attend a new, predominantly white school without the need for bussing.

lines.line: # Integration and Demographic Changes at Holcomb/Rawson School

> Upon moving to Holcomb Street, the speaker describes her initial experiences at a predominantly white school, supported by family friends and a small group of Black students. She observes the rapid diversification of the school as more Black and Jamaican children arrived, leading to significant demographic changes. The phenomenon of white flight is highlighted, with the Jewish community and other white families leaving the neighborhood and institutions like schools and churches.

lines.line: # Personal Experiences of White Flight and Neighborhood Change

> The speaker shares a poignant story about a white childhood friend whose family abruptly left the neighborhood, reflecting the broader trend of white flight. She notes the generational differences in attitudes toward integration and the sudden transformation of the neighborhood from predominantly white to Black and Jamaican. The influx of Jamaican families brought new traditions, such as competitive gardening and vibrant community life.

lines.line: # Family Gardening, Community, and Sibling Relationships

> The speaker describes her father's creativity and passion for gardening, which became a source of neighborhood pride and friendly competition. Her brother Clive, known as 'bushy,' also contributed to the community with his own garden and fruit trees. The proximity of family homes fostered a close-knit environment, and their gardening efforts became well-known in the area.

lines.line: # Timeline of School and Neighborhood Demographic Shift

> The speaker provides a timeline for her schooling, noting that she started at Rawson in 1969 when the neighborhood was still predominantly white. She observes that within a few years, the school became majority Black, culminating in a nearly all-Black graduating class by sixth grade. The rapid demographic shift is underscored by cultural changes, such as the choice of graduation songs and the near-total departure of white families.

lines.line: # Memories of the Hartford Riots and Family Involvement

> The speaker recalls the fear and anxiety during the Hartford riots, particularly concerning her father's safety as he worked late hours. She describes the curfews, the visible aftermath of unrest in her neighborhood, and her parents' active role in helping others, including bailing people out of jail. The emotional impact of these events is evident in her memories of fear, uncertainty, and the resilience of her family.

lines.line: # Family Structure, Community, and Childhood Social Life

> The speaker explains that while she was an only child in her household, her parents were community-oriented and often cared for other children. Their home was a gathering place for extended family and friends, and her parents were known for their generosity and love of children. This environment fostered a sense of belonging and community, with frequent gatherings, outings, and celebrations.

lines.line: # Father’s Stories as a Farm Worker and Experiences with Discrimination

> The speaker recounts her father's experiences as a contract farm worker in the United States, including the challenges he faced with immigration and discrimination. She shares a particularly harrowing story of near-violence and the intervention of a Barbadian ambassador, which left a lasting impression on her father. Despite hardships, her father remained optimistic and took pride in supporting his family and community.

metadata.title: Father’s Stories as a Farm Worker and Experiences with Discrimination

lines.line: # Early Impressions of Father, Family Visits to Jamaica and England

> The speaker shares a humorous anecdote about her initial reluctance to interact with her father due to his appearance, influenced by her experiences in Jamaica. She describes her first visits back to Jamaica to see her birth mother and later to England to meet her father's sisters and extended family. These trips helped her maintain connections with both sides of her family and broadened her cultural experiences.

lines.line: # Siblings, Family Migration, and Brother Clive’s Arrival

> The speaker discusses her siblings, noting that she is the youngest of seven on her mother's side and the only child between her parents. She explains that her brother Clive, about twenty years her senior, arrived in Hartford around the time the family moved to Holcomb Street. Clive was already working and soon acquired his own home nearby, further strengthening family ties in the neighborhood.

metadata.title: Siblings, Family Migration, and Brother Clive’s Arrival

lines.line: # Mr. Bennett’s Role in the Community and Family Homeownership

> Mr. Bennett played a pivotal role in helping Jamaican families, including the speaker's, purchase and retain their homes in Hartford. He acted as both realtor and insurance agent, often pre-paying insurance to ensure families did not lose their homes. His dedication and integrity left a lasting impression on the speaker, highlighting the importance of community support in immigrant success.

lines.line: # West Indian Club Activities, Parades, and Community Events

> The speaker discusses the annual cycle of West Indian Club activities, including parades, float building, and week-long celebrations that brought together people from across the region. Her father was instrumental in organizing these events, often working late into the night. The club fostered a strong sense of community and cultural pride, with the entire family participating in its many functions.

metadata.title: West Indian Club Activities, Parades, and Community Events
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the annual cycle of West Indian Club activities, including parades, float building, and week-long celebrations that brought together people from across the region. Her father was instrumental in organizing these events, often working late into the night. The club fostered a strong sense of community and cultural pride, with the entire family participating in its many functions.

lines.line: # Transition to Main Street Clubhouse and Renovation

> The speaker describes the excitement surrounding the acquisition and renovation of the new West Indian Club building on Main Street. Her father played a key role in designing and decorating the space, with the family contributing hands-on labor. The club was not just a social venue but also a center for youth enrichment, organizing trips to museums and cultural events in New York, and fostering a broader appreciation of both West Indian and American culture.

metadata.title: Transition to Main Street Clubhouse and Renovation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the excitement surrounding the acquisition and renovation of the new West Indian Club building on Main Street. Her father played a key role in designing and decorating the space, with the family contributing hands-on labor. The club was not just a social venue but also a center for youth enrichment, organizing trips to museums and cultural events in New York, and fostering a broader appreciation of both West Indian and American culture.

lines.line: # Club’s Evolution, Decline, and Reflections on Generational Change

> The speaker reflects on the evolution and eventual decline of the West Indian Social Club, noting the shift from a community-driven organization to one focused on profit and entertainment. She observes that as the club became more commercialized and less rooted in its founding values, violence and disenchantment increased, leading many dedicated members to leave. Despite these changes, the speaker and her family remain deeply connected to the club's legacy and are hopeful for its renewal.

metadata.title: Club’s Evolution, Decline, and Reflections on Generational Change
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the evolution and eventual decline of the West Indian Social Club, noting the shift from a community-driven organization to one focused on profit and entertainment. She observes that as the club became more commercialized and less rooted in its founding values, violence and disenchantment increased, leading many dedicated members to leave. Despite these changes, the speaker and her family remain deeply connected to the club's legacy and are hopeful for its renewal.

lines.line: # Father’s Career Advancement and Battle for Promotion

> The speaker recounts her father's struggle to be recognized and promoted within the Hartford Parks Department after years of performing supervisory duties without the official title. Facing institutional barriers and repeated stalling tactics, her father, with the support of family and community allies like Mr. Bennett, persisted in his fight for fair recognition. Ultimately, he became the first Black section supervisor, securing better benefits and leaving a legacy of perseverance and resilience.

children.text: # Early Childhood on Garden Street and Schooling

> The speaker recalls her early years living on Garden Street, attending Vine Street School, and forming lasting friendships, such as with Yvonne Parker. She describes the context of desegregation and the experience of being bussed to another school, which was both frightening and formative. The narrative sets the stage for her family's life and the broader social changes occurring in Hartford during her childhood.
# Father’s Involvement with the West Indian Social Club

> The speaker describes her father's deep commitment to the West Indian Social Club, where he spent much of his free time building, fixing, and organizing events. The club was central to his social life, and the family often hosted visiting bands and participated in club activities. The speaker also touches on her stepmother's social network, which revolved around card games and bingo, and her supportive but less public role in club affairs.
# The Bussing Incident and Move to Holcomb Street

> A traumatic incident occurred when the speaker, only five years old, was dropped off at the wrong location by a bus driver, leaving her stranded and frightened. An elderly woman helped her return home, and this event led her parents to withdraw her from the bussed school. The family was in the process of moving to Holcomb Street, which allowed her to attend a new, predominantly white school without the need for bussing.
# Integration and Demographic Changes at Holcomb/Rawson School

> Upon moving to Holcomb Street, the speaker describes her initial experiences at a predominantly white school, supported by family friends and a small group of Black students. She observes the rapid diversification of the school as more Black and Jamaican children arrived, leading to significant demographic changes. The phenomenon of white flight is highlighted, with the Jewish community and other white families leaving the neighborhood and institutions like schools and churches.
# Personal Experiences of White Flight and Neighborhood Change

> The speaker shares a poignant story about a white childhood friend whose family abruptly left the neighborhood, reflecting the broader trend of white flight. She notes the generational differences in attitudes toward integration and the sudden transformation of the neighborhood from predominantly white to Black and Jamaican. The influx of Jamaican families brought new traditions, such as competitive gardening and vibrant community life.
# Family Gardening, Community, and Sibling Relationships

> The speaker describes her father's creativity and passion for gardening, which became a source of neighborhood pride and friendly competition. Her brother Clive, known as 'bushy,' also contributed to the community with his own garden and fruit trees. The proximity of family homes fostered a close-knit environment, and their gardening efforts became well-known in the area.
# Timeline of School and Neighborhood Demographic Shift

> The speaker provides a timeline for her schooling, noting that she started at Rawson in 1969 when the neighborhood was still predominantly white. She observes that within a few years, the school became majority Black, culminating in a nearly all-Black graduating class by sixth grade. The rapid demographic shift is underscored by cultural changes, such as the choice of graduation songs and the near-total departure of white families.
# Memories of the Hartford Riots and Family Involvement

> The speaker recalls the fear and anxiety during the Hartford riots, particularly concerning her father's safety as he worked late hours. She describes the curfews, the visible aftermath of unrest in her neighborhood, and her parents' active role in helping others, including bailing people out of jail. The emotional impact of these events is evident in her memories of fear, uncertainty, and the resilience of her family.
# Family Structure, Community, and Childhood Social Life

> The speaker explains that while she was an only child in her household, her parents were community-oriented and often cared for other children. Their home was a gathering place for extended family and friends, and her parents were known for their generosity and love of children. This environment fostered a sense of belonging and community, with frequent gatherings, outings, and celebrations.
# Father’s Stories as a Farm Worker and Experiences with Discrimination

> The speaker recounts her father's experiences as a contract farm worker in the United States, including the challenges he faced with immigration and discrimination. She shares a particularly harrowing story of near-violence and the intervention of a Barbadian ambassador, which left a lasting impression on her father. Despite hardships, her father remained optimistic and took pride in supporting his family and community.
# Early Impressions of Father, Family Visits to Jamaica and England

> The speaker shares a humorous anecdote about her initial reluctance to interact with her father due to his appearance, influenced by her experiences in Jamaica. She describes her first visits back to Jamaica to see her birth mother and later to England to meet her father's sisters and extended family. These trips helped her maintain connections with both sides of her family and broadened her cultural experiences.
# Siblings, Family Migration, and Brother Clive’s Arrival

> The speaker discusses her siblings, noting that she is the youngest of seven on her mother's side and the only child between her parents. She explains that her brother Clive, about twenty years her senior, arrived in Hartford around the time the family moved to Holcomb Street. Clive was already working and soon acquired his own home nearby, further strengthening family ties in the neighborhood.
# Mr. Bennett’s Role in the Community and Family Homeownership

> Mr. Bennett played a pivotal role in helping Jamaican families, including the speaker's, purchase and retain their homes in Hartford. He acted as both realtor and insurance agent, often pre-paying insurance to ensure families did not lose their homes. His dedication and integrity left a lasting impression on the speaker, highlighting the importance of community support in immigrant success.
# West Indian Club Activities, Parades, and Community Events

> The speaker discusses the annual cycle of West Indian Club activities, including parades, float building, and week-long celebrations that brought together people from across the region. Her father was instrumental in organizing these events, often working late into the night. The club fostered a strong sense of community and cultural pride, with the entire family participating in its many functions.
# Transition to Main Street Clubhouse and Renovation

> The speaker describes the excitement surrounding the acquisition and renovation of the new West Indian Club building on Main Street. Her father played a key role in designing and decorating the space, with the family contributing hands-on labor. The club was not just a social venue but also a center for youth enrichment, organizing trips to museums and cultural events in New York, and fostering a broader appreciation of both West Indian and American culture.
# Club’s Evolution, Decline, and Reflections on Generational Change

> The speaker reflects on the evolution and eventual decline of the West Indian Social Club, noting the shift from a community-driven organization to one focused on profit and entertainment. She observes that as the club became more commercialized and less rooted in its founding values, violence and disenchantment increased, leading many dedicated members to leave. Despite these changes, the speaker and her family remain deeply connected to the club's legacy and are hopeful for its renewal.
# Father’s Career Advancement and Battle for Promotion

> The speaker recounts her father's struggle to be recognized and promoted within the Hartford Parks Department after years of performing supervisory duties without the official title. Facing institutional barriers and repeated stalling tactics, her father, with the support of family and community allies like Mr. Bennett, persisted in his fight for fair recognition. Ultimately, he became the first Black section supervisor, securing better benefits and leaving a legacy of perseverance and resilience.

story.description: The recording is an interview with Alice James and Fiona Verno, focusing on Alice's childhood experiences in an evolving neighborhood during the desegregation push, her family dynamics, and her father's civic engagement and challenges as a Jamaican immigrant in Hartford. Key topics include Alice's early school memories, the shift from a predominantly white to a more diverse community, and the personal impacts of societal changes, such as desegregation and local riots. The dialogue highlights her father's active role in the West Indian community and his battles for professional recognition, depicting a narrative of resilience, cultural integration, and social change.
story.title: Alice James Part 2
